topic: Coastal Landscapes / Glaciated Landscapes / Dryland Landscapes
subject: geography

items.q: What changed between the MDGs (Millennium Development Goals) and the SDGs (Sustainable Development Goals)?
items.correct: MDGs (2000-2015) targeted LICs on basic poverty/health/education; SDGs (2015-2030) apply to all countries and include environmental and inequality goals — broader, more universal scope.
items.misconception: The SDGs replaced the MDGs because the MDGs were achieved.
items.random_wrong: MDGs and SDGs are the same — just renamed.
items.why: Most MDGs were partly achieved but with major gaps. SDGs expanded scope to include HICs (which all face sustainability challenges) and added climate, inequality and oceans — reflecting a shift in development thinking.

items.correct: The area of land drained by a river and its tributaries, bounded by a watershed (high ground that separates one basin from another).
items.misconception: A drainage basin is the bowl-shaped area at the mouth of the river.
items.random_wrong: A drainage basin is the underground source of a river.
items.why: Students take 'basin' too literally as a bowl. It is the entire catchment area — often a huge region, not a depression near the river itself.

items.q: Why is ocean acidification a separate threat from ocean warming?
items.correct: Acidification occurs because oceans absorb atmospheric CO2, forming carbonic acid and lowering pH — this dissolves calcium carbonate shells and corals, independent of any warming.
items.misconception: Ocean acidification is just another name for ocean warming.
items.random_wrong: Ocean acidification is caused by acid rain falling on the sea.
items.why: These are two distinct CO2-driven changes: warming affects heat-sensitive species, acidification affects calcifiers (corals, molluscs, plankton). Even with zero warming, acidification would still threaten marine food webs.

items.q: What is post-glacial rebound an example of?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Isostatic. “Eustatic” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: 1.8m/year. “0.5m/year” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many sediment cells does England have?
items.why: The real figure is 11 cells. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
